story:
Every year, someone from my family—the Jensons—is forced into a rocket and sent to the moon as punishment for something my distant ancestor, Iris Jenson, supposedly did centuries ago to anger the moon goddess. It's a tradition based on a contract so old and faded no one can read it anymore, and hardly anyone even practices the religion behind it. Still, the entire country follows the ritual out of fear, and anyone who questions it risks being sent up as well. I always believed the whole thing was ridiculous, but I never dared say so aloud.

I spent years hiding my last name, hoping that if people didn't know I was a Jenson, they wouldn't look at me with disgust. I also hoped that being a strong student would make me too valuable to sacrifice. But none of that mattered. In the end, all they cared about was my bloodline.

When I heard the officials break into my home, terror froze me in place. I curled up and prayed they wouldn't find me, but it didn't take long before one of them yanked me out of hiding. They cuffed me, dragged me outside, and loaded me into a van bound for the ceremony—the annual celebration where everyone cheers before watching a Jenson get launched to their death.

Backstage, I could hear the crowd chattering in excitement, as if this were a festival instead of an execution. When the curtain opened and the spotlight hit me, I scanned the sea of faces. I recognized so many people: my old English teacher, my neighbor who used to bake me sweets, my childhood best friend, and worst of all, someone I had thought was my sister, Melodie. But as I stared at her smiling face, I realized the truth—she must have been adopted, or maybe I was. One of us wasn't really a Jenson by blood. That's why she could stand there clapping while I was about to die. That hurt more than anything else.

I had to look away before I broke down. Meanwhile, the mayor strutted onstage and delivered his usual dramatic speech about how bravely his team "captured" me, as if I were some monstrous criminal instead of a terrified person hiding in their own home. Then he raised the ancient contract for the crowd to see and read from the faded page in a steady voice: "Let one blood-born Jenson be borne moonward with each returning year, until the goddess releases the line." I wasn't given last words. No one cared what I had to say.

When the speech ended, guards marched me to the rocket. The cheers behind me only grew louder. Inside, they strapped me into my seat. The mayor gave me one final smug look before backing away quickly as the ground crew prepared for launch.

Then came the countdown—muffled but unmistakable. My heart felt like it would explode as the numbers ticked down. When the engines ignited, the force crushed me back in my seat. Fireworks boomed outside, celebrating my departure.

As the rocket lifted off, I knew this was really happening. I was leaving Earth, alone, because of a centuries-old curse no one fully understands anymore. And despite all my hoping, all my hiding, and all my wishing to be someone else, I was still a Jenson—so this was how my story was meant to end.
